relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jerusalem's king hears that Gibeon survived by treaty and sees the conquest map changing.
Gibeon's strength makes its peace with Israel especially threatening to neighboring kings.
Five rulers are summoned into coalition.
Their target is the city that chose survival through covenant.
The armies gather around Gibeon.
The Gibeonites call upon the oath and ask Israel to come quickly.
Joshua moves with the full fighting force.
He is told not to fear the coalition.
An all-night march becomes surprise at dawn.
The coalition breaks through confusion, pursuit, and slaughter.
Hail from heaven kills more than Israel's swords.
Joshua addresses sun and moon before Israel's eyes.
The ancient poem imagines daylight held open until vengeance is complete.
The narrator marvels that YHWH listened to a human voice in war.
The story briefly returns Joshua and Israel to Gilgal.
The five kings hide inside a cave.
Their hiding place is reported.
Stones turn the cave into a prison.
The army is told to continue pursuing those fleeing toward cities.
The slaughter continues until only fortified-city survivors remain.
Israel returns intact, and no one dares speak against it.
The cave is opened.
The five named kings are brought out.
Commanders place their feet upon captive necks in a ritual of domination.
Their courage is strengthened through the humiliation of defeated enemies.
Joshua kills and displays the kings upon trees.
At sunset the bodies are removed and sealed back into the cave.
Makkedah is captured and every life inside is destroyed.
The army turns toward Libnah.
Libnah and every life in it receive the same fate.
Lachish is besieged.
On the second day its population is killed.
Gezer's king comes to help and is destroyed with his people.
The campaign reaches Eglon.
Eglon is captured and every life is devoted to destruction.
The army climbs toward Hebron.
Hebron, its towns, king, and people are destroyed without survivor.
Joshua turns toward Debir.
Debir and its towns repeat the no-survivor pattern.
Hill, southland, lowland, and slopes are summarized through total destruction.
The campaign is mapped from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza and Goshen to Gibeon.
The narrator attributes the rapid victories to YHWH fighting for Israel.
The army returns to Gilgal.

contemplative_note: Joshua 10 contains a magnificent ancient sky-poem inside a campaign of mass death. The sun's stillness cannot be separated from what the extended day is used to complete. Preserve the poetry; refuse the vengeance, humiliation, and genocide as spiritual precedent.
